Name: rate_distortion_for_transformer_compression
Title: Rate-Distortion Analysis for Transformer Model Compression
Short Hypothesis: Transformer models can be effectively compressed by formulating the problem within a rate-distortion framework, where the trade-off between compression ratio and task performance is optimized. This approach will yield tighter bounds and more efficient compression methods compared to existing heuristic-based techniques.
Related Work: Previous work has explored the use of rate-distortion theory for neural network compression, particularly in the context of pruning and quantization (Isik et al., 2021; Bu et al., 2019). However, these studies have primarily focused on convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and have not addressed the unique challenges of transformer models, such as the attention mechanism and self-attention layers. Our proposal extends these ideas to transformers, providing a more comprehensive theoretical foundation for compression in this domain.
Abstract: Transformer models have revolutionized natural language processing (NLP) but are computationally expensive. We propose a rate-distortion framework for compressing transformer models, where the trade-off between compression ratio and task performance is explicitly optimized. By treating model compression as a lossy source coding problem, we derive achievable and converse bounds on the bits-per-weight required to preserve predictive sufficiency. We introduce a novel pruning strategy based on the information bottleneck principle and validate our approach on standard NLP tasks, demonstrating significant improvements over existing heuristic-based methods. Our work provides a theoretical foundation for efficient transformer compression, bridging the gap between information theory and practical NLP applications.
Experiments: Derive the rate-distortion function for transformer models, considering the attention mechanism and self-attention layers as key components.

Develop an information-theoretic pruning strategy that minimizes distortion while reducing the model size. This will involve estimating the mutual information between the model's internal representations and the task output.

Implement the pruning strategy on transformer models trained on standard NLP tasks (e.g., GLUE, SQuAD) and compare the compression ratios and task performance with existing methods such as magnitude pruning and distillation.
Evaluate the compression efficiency by comparing the bits-per-weight required to achieve a given level of task performance with theoretical bounds.
Analyze the impact of different distortion metrics (e.g., cross-entropy loss, accuracy) on the rate-distortion trade-off and identify the most effective metrics for transformer compression.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The theoretical bounds derived may be tight but not always practically achievable due to the complexity of transformer architectures.
Estimating mutual information in high-dimensional spaces can be challenging and may require advanced techniques or approximations.
The proposed pruning strategy may introduce additional computational overhead during training, which could be a limitation for large-scale models.
The performance of the compression method may vary across different NLP tasks and datasets, requiring further fine-tuning and validation.
